    16→import Mathlib.Algebra.Group.Pointwise.Set.Basic
    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.Submodule.Lattice
    18→import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Defs
    19→
    20→open Pointwise
    21→
    22→/--
    23→A covering system of a semiring `R` is a finite set of cosets of non-zero ideals whose union
    24→covers the whole ring. In the case where `R = ℤ`, this corresponds to a choice of finitely many
    25→congruence relations such that every integer satisfies at least one of the relations.
    26→-/
    27→structure CoveringSystem (R : Type*) [CommSemiring R] where
    28→  ι : Type
    29→  [fintypeIndex : Fintype ι]
    30→  residue : ι → R
    31→  moduli : ι → Ideal R
    32→  unionCovers : ⋃ i, ({residue i} : Set R) + (moduli i : Set R) = @Set.univ R
    33→  non_trivial : ∀ i, moduli i ≠ ⊥
    34→
    35→/--
    36→We say a covering system is strict if all the congruence relations that define it are take modulo
    37→a different ideal (or number).
    38→
    39→Note: this corresponds to the notion of a covering system that Erdos was using in
    40→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    41→-/
    42→structure StrictCoveringSystem (R : Type*) [CommSemiring R] extends CoveringSystem R where
    43→  injective_moduli : moduli.Injective
    44→
    45→namespace CoveringSystem
    46→
    47→variable {R : Type*} [CommSemiring R]
    48→
    49→def coset (c : CoveringSystem R) (i : c.ι) : Set R :=
    50→  {c.residue i} + c.moduli i
    51→
    52→@[simp]
    53→theorem iUnion_cosets (c : CoveringSystem R) : ⋃ i, c.coset i = Set.univ :=
    54→  c.unionCovers
    55→
    56→end CoveringSystem
    57→

    19→/--
    20→A perfect difference set modulo `n` is a set `D` such that the map `(a, b) ↦ a - b` from
    21→`D.offDiag` to `{x : ZMod n | x ≠ 0}` is a bijection.
    22→-/
    23→def IsPerfectDifferenceSet (D : Set ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    24→  D.offDiag.BijOn (fun (a, b) => (a - b : ZMod n)) {x : ZMod n | x ≠ 0}

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 7
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/7](https://www.erdosproblems.com/7)
    24→
    25→Erdős and Selfridge asked whether there exists a distinct covering system all of whose
    26→moduli are odd.
    27→
    28→[HoNi19] Hough, B. and Nielsen, N., _Solution of the minimum modulus problem for covering
    29→systems_. Ann. of Math. (2019).
    30→
    31→[BBMST22] Balister, P., Bollobás, B., Morris, R., Sahasrabudhe, J. and Tiba, M., _Covering
    32→systems, matchings, and odd covering systems_. (2022).
    33→
    34→Selfridge showed that this problem would have a positive answer if a covering system with
    35→pairwise non-divisible moduli existed (see Problem 586).
    36→-/
    37→
    38→/--
    39→Is there a distinct covering system all of whose moduli are odd?
    40→
    41→A **distinct covering system** (`StrictCoveringSystem`) is a finite collection of
    42→congruences $\{n \equiv a_i \pmod{m_i}\}$ where all moduli $m_i$ are pairwise distinct,
    43→covering every integer. The question asks whether such a system can exist with all
    44→moduli odd and at least 2.
    45→
    46→Known results:
    47→- [HoNi19] proved that in any distinct covering system, at least one modulus must be
    48→  divisible by 2 or 3. A simpler proof was given by [BBMST22], who also showed that the
    49→  lcm of any odd covering system's moduli must be divisible by 9 or 15.
    50→- [BBMST22] proved no odd distinct covering system exists if the moduli are additionally
    51→  required to be squarefree. The general odd case remains open.
    52→-/
    53→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    54→theorem erdos_7 : answer(sorry) ↔
    55→    ∃ c : StrictCoveringSystem ℤ,
    56→      ∀ i, ∃ (m : ℕ), Odd m ∧ 1 < m ∧ c.moduli i = Ideal.span {(m : ℤ)} := by
    57→  sorry
    58→
    59→/--
    60→There is no distinct covering system with all moduli odd and squarefree.
    61→
    62→This is a stronger variant of `erdos_7` where the moduli are additionally required to be
    63→squarefree. It was disproved by [BBMST22] Balister, Bollobás, Morris, Sahasrabudhe, and Tiba.
    64→-/
    65→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    66→theorem erdos_7_squarefree : ¬ ∃ c : StrictCoveringSystem ℤ,
    67→    ∀ i, ∃ (m : ℕ), Odd m ∧ Squarefree m ∧ 1 < m ∧
    68→      c.moduli i = Ideal.span {(m : ℤ)} := by
    69→  sorry
    70→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 8
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/8](https://www.erdosproblems.com/8)
    23→
    24→The original Erdős–Graham conjecture asked: for any finite colouring of the positive integers,
    25→must there exist a covering system all of whose moduli are monochromatic?
    26→
    27→The answer is **no**, as a consequence of Hough's theorem [Ho15] that every covering system
    28→must contain a modulus below an absolute bound (at most $10^{16}$, later improved to $616000$
    29→by Balister, Bollobás, Morris, Sahasrabudhe, and Tiba [BBMST22]). One can therefore assign a
    30→distinct colour to each integer up to this bound and a single fresh colour to all larger integers;
    31→any covering system must then contain a small modulus with a unique colour, making
    32→monochromaticity impossible.
    33→
    34→[Ho15] Hough, R. D., _The interval of covering congruences_. Ann. of Math. (2) **181** (2015),
    35→no. 1, 361–382.
    36→
    37→[BBMST22] Balister, P., Bollobás, B., Morris, R., Sahasrabudhe, J., and Tiba, M.,
    38→_The Erdős covering problem: the density of the uncovered set_. Ann. of Math. (2) **198** (2023),
    39→no. 1, 1–92.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos8
    43→
    44→/--
    45→A finite system of congruences $\{(a_i, m_i)\}$ is a **covering system** if every
    46→modulus is positive and every integer satisfies at least one congruence $n \equiv a_i \pmod{m_i}$.
    47→-/
    48→def IsCoveringSystem (S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ)) : Prop :=
    49→  S.Nonempty ∧
    50→  (∀ p ∈ S, 0 < p.2) ∧
    51→  (∀ n : ℤ, ∃ p ∈ S, (p.2 : ℤ) ∣ (n - p.1))
    52→
    53→/--
    54→All moduli in a covering system are **monochromatic** under a colouring $\chi : \mathbb{N} \to \text{Fin}\, k$
    55→if there exists a colour $c$ such that every modulus in $S$ receives colour $c$.
    56→-/
    57→def HasMonochromaticModuli {k : ℕ} (χ : ℕ → Fin k) (S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ)) : Prop :=
    58→  ∃ c : Fin k, ∀ p ∈ S, χ p.2 = c
    59→
    60→/--
    61→The Erdős–Graham conjecture asked: for any finite colouring of the positive integers,
    62→must there exist a covering system all of whose moduli are monochromatic?
    63→
    64→The answer is **no**, as a consequence of Hough's theorem [Ho15] that every covering system
    65→must contain a modulus below an absolute bound.
    66→-/
    67→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    68→theorem erdos_8 : answer(False) ↔
    69→    (∀ k : ℕ, 0 < k → ∀ χ : ℕ → Fin k,
    70→      ∃ S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ), IsCoveringSystem S ∧ HasMonochromaticModuli χ S) := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos8
    74→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 278
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/278](https://www.erdosproblems.com/278)
    23→
    24→Let $A = \{n_1, \ldots, n_r\}$ be a finite set of positive integers. For any choice of
    25→residues $a_1, \ldots, a_r$, consider the set of integers covered by the union of
    26→congruence classes $m \equiv a_i \pmod{n_i}$. The covering density is the natural density
    27→of this set (which exists since the covered set is periodic with period $\operatorname{lcm}(A)$).
    28→
    29→Open question (Erdős–Graham [ErGr80, p.28]): What is the maximum covering density
    30→achievable by a suitable choice of the $a_i$?
    31→
    32→Settled (Simpson [Si86]): The minimum covering density is achieved when all $a_i$
    33→are equal, and equals the inclusion-exclusion sum
    34→$$\sum_i \frac{1}{n_i} - \sum_{i<j} \frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(n_i,n_j)}
    35→  + \sum_{i<j<k} \frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(n_i,n_j,n_k)} - \cdots$$
    36→
    37→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial
    38→number theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathématique (1980).
    39→
    40→[Si86] Simpson, R. J., *Exact coverings of the integers by arithmetic progressions*.
    41→Discrete Mathematics 59 (1986), 181–190.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→open Classical
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos278
    47→
    48→/-- The covering density of congruences $m \equiv \text{offsets}(n) \pmod{n}$ for
    49→$n \in \text{moduli}$. Since the covered set is periodic with period
    50→$L = \operatorname{lcm}(\text{moduli})$, the density equals
    51→$|\{m \in \{0,\ldots,L-1\} :
    52→\exists n \in \text{moduli},\, n \mid (m - \text{offsets}(n))\}| / L$. -/
    53→noncomputable def coveringDensity (moduli : Finset ℕ) (offsets : ℕ → ℤ) : ℚ :=
    54→  let L := moduli.lcm id
    55→  let covered := (Finset.range L).filter fun (m : ℕ) =>
    56→    ∃ n ∈ moduli, (n : ℤ) ∣ ((m : ℤ) - offsets n)
    57→  (covered.card : ℚ) / (L : ℚ)
    58→
    59→/-- The inclusion-exclusion density for a set of moduli: the alternating sum
    60→$\sum_{\emptyset \neq S \subseteq \text{moduli}} (-1)^{|S|+1} / \operatorname{lcm}(S)$.
    61→This equals the covering density when all offsets are equal. -/
    62→noncomputable def inclusionExclusionDensity (moduli : Finset ℕ) : ℚ :=
    63→  ((moduli.powerset.filter fun S => S.Nonempty).sum fun S =>
    64→    (-1 : ℚ) ^ (S.card + 1) / ((S.lcm id : ℕ) : ℚ))
    65→
    66→/--
    67→Erdős Problem 278 (Simpson's theorem [Si86]):
    68→
    69→Is the minimum covering density, over all choices of offsets for a finite set of
    70→positive integer moduli, achieved when all offsets are equal (giving the
    71→inclusion-exclusion density)?
    72→
    73→Answered affirmatively by Simpson [Si86].
    74→-/
    75→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    76→theorem erdos_278 : answer(True) ↔
    77→    ∀ (moduli : Finset ℕ), (∀ n ∈ moduli, 0 < n) →
    78→      (∀ offsets : ℕ → ℤ,
    79→        inclusionExclusionDensity moduli ≤
    80→          coveringDensity moduli offsets) ∧
    81→      (∀ a : ℤ,
    82→        coveringDensity moduli (fun _ => a) =
    83→          inclusionExclusionDensity moduli) := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→/-- Open question (Erdős–Graham [ErGr80, p.28]): What is the maximum
    87→covering density achievable by a suitable choice of offsets? -/
    88→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    89→theorem erdos_278.variants.max_density
    90→    (moduli : Finset ℕ) (h : ∀ n ∈ moduli, 0 < n) :
    91→    IsGreatest {coveringDensity moduli offsets |
    92→      offsets : ℕ → ℤ} (answer(sorry)) := by
    93→  sorry
    94→
    95→end Erdos278
    96→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 586
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/586](https://www.erdosproblems.com/586)
    23→
    24→Asked by Schinzel, motivated by a question of Erdős and Selfridge.
    25→The answer is no, as proved by Balister, Bollobás, Morris, Sahasrabudhe,
    26→and Tiba [BBMST22].
    27→
    28→[BBMST22] Balister, P., Bollobás, B., Morris, R., Sahasrabudhe, J., and Tiba, M.,
    29→_On the Erdős covering problem: the density of the uncovered set_, Inventiones
    30→mathematicae (2022).
    31→-/
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos586
    34→
    35→/-- A covering system: a finite collection of residue classes $a_i \bmod n_i$ (with $n_i \geq 2$)
    36→    that covers every integer. -/
    37→def IsCoveringSystem (k : ℕ) (a : Fin k → ℤ) (n : Fin k → ℕ) : Prop :=
    38→  (∀ i : Fin k, 2 ≤ n i) ∧
    39→  (∀ z : ℤ, ∃ i : Fin k, (n i : ℤ) ∣ (z - a i))
    40→
    41→/-- **Erdős Problem 586** (Schinzel): Is there a covering system such that no two of the
    42→    moduli divide each other? The answer is no, as proved by Balister, Bollobás, Morris,
    43→    Sahasrabudhe, and Tiba [BBMST22]. -/
    44→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    45→theorem erdos_586 : answer(False) ↔
    46→    (∃ (k : ℕ) (a : Fin k → ℤ) (n : Fin k → ℕ),
    47→      IsCoveringSystem k a n ∧
    48→      ∀ i j : Fin k, i ≠ j → ¬(n i ∣ n j)) := by
    49→  sorry
    50→
    51→end Erdos586
    52→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 27
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/27](https://www.erdosproblems.com/27)
    23→
    24→An $\varepsilon$-almost covering system is a set of congruences $a_i \pmod{n_i}$ for distinct
    25→moduli $n_1 < \cdots < n_k$ such that the natural density of integers satisfying none of them
    26→is at most $\varepsilon$.
    27→
    28→Erdős asked whether there exists a constant $C > 1$ such that for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and
    29→$N \geq 1$, there is an $\varepsilon$-almost covering system with $N \leq n_1 < \cdots < n_k \leq CN$.
    30→This was disproved by Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance, and Yu [FFKPY07].
    31→
    32→[FFKPY07] Filaseta, M., Ford, K., Konyagin, S., Pomerance, C., and Yu, G.,
    33→_Sieving by large integers and covering systems of congruences_.
    34→Journal of the American Mathematical Society (2007), 495-517.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open Finset
    38→
    39→open scoped Classical
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos27
    42→
    43→/-- A congruence system has distinct moduli if no two pairs share the same modulus. -/
    44→def HasDistinctModuli (S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ)) : Prop :=
    45→  S.card = (S.image Prod.snd).card
    46→
    47→/-- The LCM of all moduli in a congruence system. -/
    48→noncomputable def systemLcm (S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ)) : ℕ :=
    49→  (S.image Prod.snd).lcm id
    50→
    51→/--
    52→The density of uncovered integers for a congruence system, measured as the proportion of
    53→integers in $\{0, \ldots, L-1\}$ not covered by any congruence, where $L$ is the LCM of
    54→all moduli.
    55→-/
    56→noncomputable def uncoveredDensity (S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ)) : ℝ :=
    57→  let L := systemLcm S
    58→  ((Finset.range L).filter (fun x =>
    59→    ∀ p ∈ S, ¬((↑p.2 : ℤ) ∣ (↑x - p.1)))).card / (L : ℝ)
    60→
    61→/--
    62→There is no constant $C > 1$ such that for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and $N \geq 1$, there
    63→exists an $\varepsilon$-almost covering system whose moduli all lie in the interval $[N, CN]$.
    64→
    65→Erdős conjectured this was possible; it was disproved by Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin,
    66→Pomerance, and Yu [FFKPY07].
    67→-/
    68→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    69→theorem erdos_27 :
    70→    answer(False) ↔
    71→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 1 ∧
    72→      ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    73→      ∀ N : ℕ, N ≥ 1 →
    74→      ∃ S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ),
    75→        HasDistinctModuli S ∧
    76→        S.Nonempty ∧
    77→        (∀ p ∈ S, p.2 ≥ 2) ∧
    78→        (∀ p ∈ S, N ≤ p.2 ∧ (p.2 : ℝ) ≤ C * N) ∧
    79→        uncoveredDensity S ≤ ε := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→end Erdos27
    83→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 202
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/202](https://www.erdosproblems.com/202)
    23→
    24→Let $n_1 < \cdots < n_r \leq N$ with associated $a_i \pmod{n_i}$ such that the congruence
    25→classes are disjoint (every integer is $\equiv a_i \pmod{n_i}$ for at most one $i$).
    26→How large can $r$ be in terms of $N$?
    27→
    28→Let $f(N)$ denote the maximum such $r$. Erdős and Stein conjectured $f(N) = o(N)$,
    29→proved by Erdős and Szemerédi [ErSz68]. The exact asymptotics remain open.
    30→
    31→The best known bounds, due to de la Bretèche, Ford, and Vandehey [BFV13], are:
    32→$$N / L(N)^{1+o(1)} < f(N) < N / L(N)^{\sqrt{3}/2+o(1)}$$
    33→where $L(N) = \exp(\sqrt{\log N \cdot \log \log N})$.
    34→
    35→They conjecture the lower bound is the truth, i.e., $f(N) = N / L(N)^{1+o(1)}$.
    36→
    37→[ErSz68] Erdős, P. and Szemerédi, E.
    38→
    39→[BFV13] de la Bretèche, R., Ford, K., and Vandehey, J.
    40→
    41→[Er61, Er65, Er65b, Er73, Er77c, ErGr80, Va99] — see
    42→[erdosproblems.com/202](https://www.erdosproblems.com/202) for full references.
    43→-/
    44→
    45→open Filter
    46→
    47→namespace Erdos202
    48→
    49→/-- A system of congruence classes with distinct positive moduli at most $N$ is
    50→*pairwise disjoint* if no integer belongs to more than one class.
    51→Each element $(n, a)$ represents the congruence class
    52→$\{x \in \mathbb{Z} : x \equiv a \pmod{n}\}$. -/
    53→def IsPairwiseDisjointSystem (S : Finset (ℕ × ℤ)) (N : ℕ) : Prop :=
    54→  (∀ p ∈ S, 1 ≤ p.1 ∧ p.1 ≤ N) ∧
    55→  (∀ p ∈ S, ∀ q ∈ S, p ≠ q → p.1 ≠ q.1) ∧
    56→  (∀ p ∈ S, ∀ q ∈ S, p ≠ q →
    57→    ∀ x : ℤ, ¬(x % (↑p.1 : ℤ) = p.2 % (↑p.1 : ℤ) ∧
    58→               x % (↑q.1 : ℤ) = q.2 % (↑q.1 : ℤ)))
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 202 (de la Bretèche–Ford–Vandehey conjecture) — OPEN.
    62→[Er61, Er65, Er65b, Er73, Er77c, ErGr80, Va99]:
    63→
    64→The maximum number $f(N)$ of pairwise disjoint congruence classes with distinct
    65→moduli at most $N$ satisfies $f(N) \leq N / L(N)^{1-\varepsilon}$ for every
    66→$\varepsilon > 0$ and all sufficiently large $N$, where
    67→$L(N) = \exp(\sqrt{\log N \cdot \log \log N})$.
    68→
    69→Combined with the proved lower bound $f(N) \geq N / L(N)^{1+\varepsilon}$ [BFV13], this
    70→gives the conjectured asymptotics $f(N) = N / L(N)^{1+o(1)}$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    73→theorem erdos_202 :
    74→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    75→    ∀ᶠ N : ℕ in atTop,
    76→    ∀ S : Finset (ℕ × ℤ),
    77→    IsPairwiseDisjointSystem S N →
    78→    (S.card : ℝ) ≤ (N : ℝ) /
    79→      Real.exp ((1 - ε) * Real.sqrt (Real.log (N : ℝ) * Real.log (Real.log (N : ℝ)))) := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→end Erdos202
    83→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 204
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [erdosproblems.com/204](https://www.erdosproblems.com/204)
    24→- [Ad25] S. Adenwalla, A Question of Erdős and Graham on Covering Systems. arXiv:2501.15170 (2025).
    25→-/
    26→
    27→namespace Erdos204
    28→
    29→/--
    30→Are there $n$ such that there is a covering system with moduli the divisors of $n$ which is 'as
    31→disjoint as possible'?
    32→
    33→That is, for all $d\mid n$ with $d>1$ there is an associated $a_d$ such that every integer is
    34→congruent to some $a_d\pmod{d}$, and if there is some integer $x$ with
    35→\[x\equiv a_d\pmod{d}\textrm{ and }x\equiv a_{d'}\pmod{d'}\]then $(d,d')=1$.
    36→
    37→The density of such $n$ is zero. Erdős and Graham believed that no such $n$ exist.
    38→
    39→Adenwalla [Ad25] has proved there are no such $n$.
    40→-/
    41→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    42→theorem erdos_204 : answer(False) ↔ ∃ (n : ℕ) (a : ℕ → ℤ),
    43→    let D := {d : ℕ | d ∣ n ∧ d > 1}
    44→    (∀ x : ℤ, ∃ d ∈ D, x ≡ a d [ZMOD d]) ∧
    45→    (∀ d ∈ D, ∀ d' ∈ D, d ≠ d' → (∃ x : ℤ, x ≡ a d [ZMOD d] → x ≡ a d' [ZMOD d']) →
    46→      Nat.gcd d d' = 1) := by
    47→  sorry
    48→
    49→end Erdos204
    50→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2025 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.NatInt
    18→import Mathlib.Data.ENat.Lattice
    19→import Mathlib.Data.Set.Card
    20→import Mathlib.Tactic.IntervalCases
    21→
    22→open scoped Classical
    23→
    24→/-! # Arithmetic Progressions
    25→
    26→Main definitions:
    27→- `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) (a d : α)` : predicate asserting that `s` is the
    28→  set consisting of an arithmetic progression of length `l` (possibly infinite) with first term
    29→  `a` and difference `d`. Useful for cases in which additional conditions need to be applied to
    30→  the individual terms and/or difference.
    31→- `Set.IsAPOfLength (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞)` : predicate asserting that `s` is the set consisting
    32→  of an arithmetic progression of length `l`, for some some first term and difference.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→variable {α : Type*} [AddCommMonoid α]
    36→
    37→/--
    38→A set $S$ is an arithmetic progression of length $l$ with first term $a$ and difference $d$
    39→if $S = \{a, a + d, ..., a + (l - 1)d\}$, if $l$ if finite, else $S = \{a, a + d, a + 2d, ...\}.
    40→This can be written as `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith a l a d`, where `l : ℕ∞` may take the infinite
    41→value `⊤`.
    42→-/
    43→def Set.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) (a d : α) : Prop :=
    44→  ENat.card s = l ∧ s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)}
    45→
    46→/--
    47→A list version of `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith`. Useful when order preservation is required, for example
    48→when considering images under arbitrary functions.
    49→-/
    50→def List.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : List α) (l : ℕ) (a d : α) : Prop :=
    51→  s = (List.range l).map (fun n ↦ a + n • d) ∨ s = (List.range l).reverse.map (fun n ↦ a + n • d)
    52→
    53→namespace Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
    54→
    55→variable {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞} {a d : α}
    56→
    57→theorem card (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : ENat.card s = l := h.1
    58→theorem eq (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)} := h.2
    59→
    60→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length zero if and only
    61→if `s` is empty. -/
    62→@[simp]
    63→theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 0 a d ↔ s = ∅ := by simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    64→
    65→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length one if and only
    66→if `s` is a singleton. -/
    67→@[simp]
    68→theorem one : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 1 a d ↔ s = {a} := by simp +contextual [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    69→
    70→end Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
    71→
    72→namespace List.IsAPOfLengthWith
    73→
    74→variable {s : List α} {l : ℕ} {a d : α}
    75→
    76→theorem length (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : s.length = l := by
    77→  cases h <;> simp_all
    78→
    79→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length zero if and only
    80→if `s` is empty. -/
    81→@[simp]
    82→theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 0 a d ↔ s = [] := by
    83→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    84→
    85→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length one if and only
    86→if `s` is a singleton. -/
    87→@[simp]
    88→theorem one : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 1 a d ↔ s = [a] := by
    89→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    90→
    91→end List.IsAPOfLengthWith
    92→
    93→/-- In an abelian additive group `α`, the set `{a, b}` with `a ≠ b` is an arithmetic progression of
    94→length `2` with first term `a` and difference `b - a`. -/
    95→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthWith_pair {α : Type*} [DecidableEq α] [AddCommGroup α]
    96→    {a b : α} (hab : a ≠ b) :
    97→    Set.IsAPOfLengthWith {a, b} 2 a (b - a) := by
    98→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    99→  rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem (by simpa only [Finset.mem_singleton])]
   100→  simp
   101→  refine Set.ext fun x => ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨n, ⟨_, _⟩⟩ ↦ by interval_cases n <;> simp_all⟩
   102→  cases h with
   103→  | inl hl => use 0; simp [hl]
   104→  | inr hr => exact ⟨1, by norm_num, by simp_all⟩
   105→
   106→-- Formalisation note: separate result needed for `ℕ` since this is not covered by
   107→-- the `AddCommGroup` result above.
   108→/-- The set `{a, b} : Set ℕ` with `a < b` is an arithmetic progression of length `2` with
   109→first term `a` and difference `b - a`. -/
   110→theorem Nat.isAPOfLengthWith_pair {a b : ℕ} (hab : a < b) :
   111→    Set.IsAPOfLengthWith {a, b} 2 a (b - a) := by
   112→  let ⟨n, h⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_add_of_lt hab
   113→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthWith, h, add_assoc]
   114→  exact Set.ext fun x => ⟨fun a => by aesop, fun ⟨w, ⟨_, _⟩⟩ => by interval_cases w <;> simp_all⟩
   115→
   116→/--
   117→The predicate that a set `s` is an arithmetic progression of length `l` (possibly infinite).
   118→This predicate does not assert a specific value for the first term or the difference of the
   119→arithmetic progression.
   120→-/
   121→def Set.IsAPOfLength (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) : Prop :=
   122→  ∃ a d : α, s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d
   123→
   124→/--
   125→A list version of `Set.IsAPOfLength`. Useful when order preservation is required, for example
   126→when considering images under arbitrary functions.
   127→-/
   128→def List.IsAPOfLength (s : List α) (l : ℕ) : Prop :=
   129→  ∃ a d : α, s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d
   130→
   131→namespace Set.IsAPOfLength
   132→
   133→open Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
   134→
   135→variable {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞}
   136→
   137→theorem card (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : ENat.card s = l := h.choose_spec.choose_spec.1
   138→
   139→theorem eq (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : ∃ a d : α, s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)} :=
   140→  ⟨h.choose, h.choose_spec.choose, h.choose_spec.choose_spec.2⟩
   141→
   142→/-- Only the empty set is a finite arithmetic progression of length $0$. -/
   143→@[simp] theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLength 0 ↔ s = ∅ := by simp [Set.IsAPOfLength]
   144→
   145→/-- Only singletons are finite arithmetic progressions of length $1$. -/
   146→@[simp] theorem one : s.IsAPOfLength 1 ↔ ∃ a, s = {a} := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   147→
   148→/-- If a set is an arithmetic progression of lengths `l₁` and `l₂`, then the lengths are
   149→equal. -/
   150→theorem congr {s : Set α} {l₁ l₂ : ℕ∞}
   151→    (h₁ : s.IsAPOfLength l₁) (h₂ : s.IsAPOfLength l₂) :
   152→    l₁ = l₂ := by
   153→  rw [← h₁.card, h₂.card]
   154→
   155→end Set.IsAPOfLength
   156→
   157→namespace List.IsAPOfLength
   158→
   159→open List.IsAPOfLengthWith
   160→
   161→variable {s : List α} {l : ℕ}
   162→
   163→theorem length (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : s.length = l := by
   164→  obtain ⟨_, _, h⟩ := h
   165→  exact h.length
   166→
   167→/-- Only the empty list is a finite arithmetic progression of length $0$. -/
   168→@[simp] theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLength 0 ↔ s = [] := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   169→
   170→/-- Only singletons are finite arithmetic progressions of length $1$. -/
   171→@[simp] theorem one : s.IsAPOfLength 1 ↔ ∃ a, s = [a] := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   172→
   173→/-- If a list is an arithmetic progression of lengths `l₁` and `l₂`, then the lengths are
   174→equal. -/
   175→theorem congr {s : List α} {l₁ l₂ : ℕ}
   176→    (h₁ : s.IsAPOfLength l₁) (h₂ : s.IsAPOfLength l₂) :
   177→    l₁ = l₂ := by
   178→  rw [← h₁.length, h₂.length]
   179→
   180→end List.IsAPOfLength
   181→
   182→theorem Set.isAPOfLength_pair {α : Type*} [DecidableEq α] [AddCommGroup α] {a b : α} (hab : a ≠ b) :
   183→    Set.IsAPOfLength {a, b} 2 :=
   184→  ⟨a, b - a, Set.isAPOfLengthWith_pair hab⟩
   185→
   186→theorem Nat.isAPOfLength_pair {a b : ℕ} (hab : a < b) :
   187→    Set.IsAPOfLength {a, b} 2 :=
   188→  ⟨a, b - a, Nat.isAPOfLengthWith_pair hab⟩
   189→
   190→/-- The empty set is not an arithmetic progression of positive length. -/
   191→theorem Set.not_isAPOfLength_empty {l : ℕ∞} (hl : 0 < l) :
   192→    ¬Set.IsAPOfLength (∅ : Set α) l :=
   193→  fun h ↦ by simp_all [h.congr <| Set.IsAPOfLength.zero.2 rfl]
   194→
   195→/-- We say that a set `s` is free of arithmetic progressions of length `l` if `s` contains no
   196→non-trivial arithmetic progressions of length `l`. Written as `Set.IsAPOfLengthFree s l`. --/
   197→def Set.IsAPOfLengthFree (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) : Prop :=
   198→  ∀ t ⊆ s, t.IsAPOfLength l → l ≤ 1
   199→
   200→/-- Any set is free of arithmetic progressions of length `1`, because such APs are all trivial. -/
   201→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthFree_one (s : Set α) : s.IsAPOfLengthFree 1 := by
   202→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree]
   203→
   204→/-- Any set is free of arithmetic progressions of length `0`, because such APs are all trivial. -/
   205→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthFree_zero (s : Set α) : s.IsAPOfLengthFree 0 := by
   206→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree]
   207→
   208→/-- Any non-trivial arithmetic progression cannot be free of arithmetic progressions. -/
   209→theorem Set.IsAPOfLength.not_isAPOfLengthFree {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞}
   210→    (hs : s.IsAPOfLength l) (hl : 1 < l) : ¬s.IsAPOfLengthFree l := by
   211→  simpa [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree] using ⟨s, le_rfl, ⟨hs, hl⟩⟩
   212→
   213→/--
   214→Define the largest possible size of a subset of $\{1, \dots, N\}$ that does not contain
   215→any non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression.
   216→-/
   217→noncomputable def Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard (k : ℕ) (N : ℕ) : ℕ :=
   218→  sSup {Finset.card S | (S) (_ : S ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N) (_ : (S : Set ℕ).IsAPOfLengthFree k)}
   219→
   220→theorem Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard_zero (N : ℕ) : maxCard 0 N = N := by
   221→  simp only [maxCard, Nat.cast_zero, isAPOfLengthFree_zero, exists_const, exists_prop]
   222→  apply IsGreatest.csSup_eq
   223→  refine ⟨⟨Finset.Icc 1 N, Subset.rfl, Nat.card_Icc _ _⟩, ?_⟩
   224→  rintro n ⟨S, hS, rfl⟩
   225→  exact S.card_mono hS |>.trans_eq (Nat.card_Icc _ _)
   226→
   227→theorem Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard_one (N : ℕ) : maxCard 1 N = N := by
   228→  nth_rw 2 [← maxCard_zero N]
   229→  simp [maxCard, isAPOfLengthFree_one, isAPOfLengthFree_zero]
   230→
   231→/-- A set `A` contains an arithmetic progression of length `k` with difference `d`. -/
   232→def Set.ContainsAP (A : Set α) (k : ℕ) (d : α) : Prop :=
   233→  ∃ a, ∃ s, s ⊆ A ∧ s.IsAPOfLengthWith (k : ℕ∞) a d
   234→
   235→def ContainsMonoAPofLength {κ : Type} [Finite κ] {M : Set α}
   236→    (coloring : M → κ) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
   237→  ∃ c : κ, ∃ ap : Set M, ((·.1) '' ap).IsAPOfLength k ∧
   238→    ∀ m ∈ ap, coloring m = c
   239→
   240→/--
   241→A function `f : α → β` has a monotone `k`-term arithmetic progression if there exists an
   242→arithmetic progression `l` of length `k` in `α` such that its image under `f` is sorted.
   243→-/
   244→def HasMonotoneAP {β : Type*} [Preorder β] (f : α → β) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
   245→  ∃ l : List α, l.IsAPOfLength k ∧ (l.map f).Pairwise (· < ·)
   246→
   247→/--
   248→Define the largest possible size of a subset of a finset `s` that does not contain
   249→any non-trivial `k`-term arithmetic progression.
   250→-/
   251→noncomputable def Finset.maxAPFreeCard (k : ℕ) (s : Finset α) : ℕ :=
   252→  (s.powerset.filter fun t : Finset α ↦ (t : Set α).IsAPOfLengthFree k).sup Finset.card
   253→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 274
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→[erdosproblems.com/274](https://www.erdosproblems.com/274)
    24→[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Herzog%E2%80%93Sch%C3%B6nheim_conjecture)
    25→-/
    26→
    27→open scoped Pointwise Cardinal
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos274
    30→
    31→-- TODO(callesonne): add already proved results from the wiki page
    32→
    33→/-- An exact covering of a group `G` is a finite collection of subgroups `{H_1, ..., H_k}` and
    34→representative `{g_1, ..., g_k}` such that the cosets `g_iH_i` are pairwise disjoint and their
    35→union covers `G`.
    36→
    37→Note that this differs from `Partition (α := Subgroup G)` because the covering condition there
    38→invokes `Subgroup.sup` which is subgroup generation and thus stronger than union. This definition
    39→is easier to use in this contect than the alternative `Partition (α := Set G)`, which lacks
    40→subgroup definitions such as `Subgroup.index`. -/
    41→structure Group.ExactCovering (G : Type*) [Group G] (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
    42→  parts : ι → Subgroup G
    43→  reps : ι → G
    44→  nonempty (i : ι) : (parts i : Set G).Nonempty
    45→  disjoint : (Set.univ (α := ι)).PairwiseDisjoint fun (i : ι) ↦ reps i • (parts i : Set G)
    46→  covers : ⋃ i, reps i • (parts i : Set G) = Set.univ
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Does there exist a group `G` with an exact covering by more than one cosets of
    50→different sizes? (i.e. each element is contained in exactly one of the cosets.)
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 20]
    53→theorem erdos_274 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ (G : Type*) (h : Group G) (hG : 1 < ENat.card G)
    54→    (ι : Type*) (_ : Fintype ι) (P : Group.ExactCovering G ι),
    55→      1 < Fintype.card ι ∧ (Set.range P.parts).Pairwise fun A B ↦ #A ≠ #B := by
    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→/--
    59→If `G` is a finite abelian group then there cannot exist an exact covering of `G` by more
    60→than one cosets of different sizes? (i.e. each element is contained in exactly one
    61→of the cosets.)
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    64→theorem erdos_274.variants.abelian {G : Type*} [Fintype G] [CommGroup G]
    65→    (hG : 1 < Fintype.card G) {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (P : Group.ExactCovering G ι)
    66→    (hι : 1 < Fintype.card ι) :
    67→    ∃ i j, i ≠ j ∧ #(P.parts i) = #(P.parts j) := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→/--
    71→Let $G$ be a group, and let $A = \{a_1G_1, \dots, a_kG_k\}$ be a finite system of left cosets of
    72→subgroups $G_1, \dots, G_k$ of $G$.
    73→
    74→Herzog and Schönheim conjectured that if $A$ forms a partition of $G$ with $k > 1$, then the
    75→indices $[G:G_1], \dots, [G:G_k]$ cannot be distinct.
    76→-/
    77→@[category research open, AMS 20]
    78→theorem herzog_schonheim {G : Type*} [Group G] (hG : 1 < ENat.card G) {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι]
    79→    (hι : 1 < Fintype.card ι) (P : Group.ExactCovering G ι) :
    80→    ∃ i j, i ≠ j ∧ (P.parts i).index = (P.parts j).index := by
    81→  sorry
    82→

    20→# Erdős Problem 2
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/2](https://www.erdosproblems.com/2)
    23→
    24→Erdős asked whether the smallest modulus of a covering system can be arbitrarily large.
    25→Hough [Hou15] showed the answer is no; the best bounds are due to Balister, Bollobás,
    26→Morris, Sahasrabudhe, and Tiba [BBMST22].
    27→
    28→[Hou15] Hough, R. D., _Solution of the minimum modulus problem for covering systems_.
    29→Annals of Mathematics (2015), 361-382.
    30→
    31→[FFKPY07] Filaseta, M., Ford, K., Konyagin, S., Pomerance, C., and Yu, G., _Sieving by
    32→large integers and covering systems of congruences_. Journal of the American Mathematical
    33→Society (2007), 495-517.
    34→
    35→[BBMST22] Balister, P., Bollobás, B., Morris, R., Sahasrabudhe, J., and Tiba, M.,
    36→_The Erdős covering conjecture_. Annals of Mathematics (2022).
    37→
    38→[Ow14] Owens, T., _A covering system with minimum modulus 42_. (2014).
    39→-/
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos2
    42→
    43→/--
    44→A finite system of congruences $\{(a_i, m_i)\}$ is a **covering system** if every
    45→modulus is positive and every integer satisfies at least one congruence $n \equiv a_i \pmod{m_i}$.
    46→-/
    47→def IsCoveringSystem (S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ)) : Prop :=
    48→  S.Nonempty ∧
    49→  (∀ p ∈ S, 0 < p.2) ∧
    50→  (∀ n : ℤ, ∃ p ∈ S, (p.2 : ℤ) ∣ (n - p.1))
    51→
    52→/--
    53→Erdős asked whether the smallest modulus of a covering system can be arbitrarily
    54→large (he expected the answer to be yes). Hough [Hou15], building on work of
    55→Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance, and Yu [FFKPY07], showed the answer is **no**:
    56→every covering system has smallest modulus at most $10^{16}$. Balister, Bollobás,
    57→Morris, Sahasrabudhe, and Tiba [BBMST22] gave a simpler proof and improved the
    58→bound to $616000$. The best known lower bound is $42$, due to Owens [Ow14].
    59→
    60→Formally: there exists an absolute constant $B$ such that every covering system
    61→contains a congruence whose modulus is at most $B$.
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    64→theorem erdos_2 :
    65→    ∃ B : ℕ, ∀ S : Finset (ℤ × ℕ), IsCoveringSystem S →
    66→      ∃ p ∈ S, p.2 ≤ B := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→end Erdos2

## Summary Report: Covering Systems, Residue Classes, and Modular Arithmetic Code

I have completed a thorough search of the FormalConjectures and FormalConjecturesForMathlib directories for code related to covering systems, residue classes, modular arithmetic, and related topics. Here are the key findings:

### PRIMARY RESOURCE: Covering System Definition

**File:** `/home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjecturesForMathlib/NumberTheory/CoveringSystem.lean`

This is the main reusable library file containing the formal definitions of covering systems:

```lean
structure CoveringSystem (R : Type*) [CommSemiring R] where
  ι : Type
  [fintypeIndex : Fintype ι]
  residue : ι → R
  moduli : ι → Ideal R
  unionCovers : ⋃ i, ({residue i} : Set R) + (moduli i : Set R) = @Set.univ R
  non_trivial : ∀ i, moduli i ≠ ⊥

structure StrictCoveringSystem (R : Type*) [CommSemiring R] extends CoveringSystem R where
  injective_moduli : moduli.Injective

def coset (c : CoveringSystem R) (i : c.ι) : Set R :=
  {c.residue i} + c.moduli i

theorem iUnion_cosets (c : CoveringSystem R) : ⋃ i, c.coset i = Set.univ :=
  c.unionCovers
```

A `CoveringSystem` is defined over a general commutative semiring and uses ideals to represent the congruences. A `StrictCoveringSystem` adds the constraint that all moduli are distinct (injective).

### PERFECT DIFFERENCE SET DEFINITION

**File:** `/home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Data/ZMod/PerfectDifferenceSet.lean`

```lean
def IsPerfectDifferenceSet (D : Set ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
  D.offDiag.BijOn (fun (a, b) => (a - b : ZMod n)) {x : ZMod n | x ≠ 0}
```

This defines perfect difference sets using ZMod for residue class arithmetic.
